coredata.dc:title: Production of high biocompatibility fluorescent polyamide fabrics using carbon dots derived from natural Malabar spinach seeds
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Hou, Lihui
Ge, Shuping
Li, Weihua
Liu, Qiulian
Liu, Jin
Li, Jingyi
Qu, Changqing
Zhang, Yuanming
Zhao, Haiguang
Jiang, Wei
Han, Yi
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted great attention due to their structurally tunable absorption/emission, biocompatibility, low-cost and facile synthetic methods. Although many types of commercial chemicals have been used as precursors to produce C-dots, it is a challenge to obtain C-dots with good biocompatibility and brightness for textile applications. In this study, we produced colloidal C-dots by using Malabar spinach seeds juice (MSSJ) as a natural precursor, demonstrating the impact of the seeds' chemical composition on C-dots formation and performance. MSSJ contains carbohydrates, protein, glycerol, ethanol, chlorophyll as well as anthocyanin. Based on the variation in composition before and after C-dots synthesis, we determined that the structure of the C-dots is primarily derived from carbohydrates and protein. While chlorophyll remains stable during synthesis at 160 °C. The high nitrogen content from proteins enhances C-dots formation. The C-dots in this work have a high quantum yield of 7.8 %, and the emission peak range is 400-525 nm, which is significantly higher than C-dots derived from traditional biomass. Biological tests show no significant biotoxicity at any concentration, ensuring safe use in fluorescent textiles. This study underscores the importance of biomass-derived precursors in green C-dots synthesis, advancing the development of sustainable and biocompatible fluorescent fibers.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots (C-dots)
Biomass
Liquid chromatography
Malabar spinach seeds
Fluorescent polyamide fabric

Analysis of the Malabar spinach seed juice
Component analysis of Malabar spinach seed juice
Structure and optical properties of C-dots

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The scheme for analysis and preparation of the C-dots.

(a) TEM image of C-dots (160 °C). (b) XPS survey spectrum of the C-dots (160 °C). (c) The high-resolution C l s spectrum of the C-dots (160 °C). (d) TEM image of C-dots (200 °C). (e) XPS survey spectrum of the C-dots (200 °C). (f) The high-resolution C l s spectrum of the C-dots (200 °C). Insets of (a) and (d) are high resolution TEM of one C-dot.
(a) Ultraviolet absorption spectra. (b) PL spectra of the C-dots (160 °C). (c) C-dots (200 °C), and. (d) Chl. (160 °C).
(a) Fluorescence decay curves of the Chl., Chl. (160 °C), C-dots (160 °C) and C-dots (200 °C). (b) The QY of the Chl. (160 °C) and (c) C-dots (160 °C).
(a) Cell viability of the C-dots with different concentrations (100–500 μg/mL) for 48 h incubation. (b) Fluorescence, (c) Bright field images of the C-dots and cells. The scale bar is 50 μm.
(a) The dyeing effect of the C-dots on nylon fabric, (b) The fluorescent pattern printed by the C-dots on non-fluorescent paper.
MSSJ, C-dots (160 °C) and C-dots (200 °C) Composition changes between samples.

head.ce:title: Production of high biocompatibility fluorescent polyamide fabrics using carbon dots derived from natural Malabar spinach seeds

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, College of Materials Science and Engineering, College of Physics, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles
College of Materials Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: aCollege of Textiles & Clothing, State Key Laboratory of Bio-Fibers and Eco-Textiles, College of Materials Science and Engineering, College of Physics, College of Life Sciences, Qingdao University, Qingdao, 266071, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Kezhou Runhua Textile Technology Co. Ltd

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Kezhou Runhua Textile Technology Co. Ltd

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: bKezhou Runhua Textile Technology Co. Ltd

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (C-dots) have attracted great attention due to their structurally tunable absorption/emission, biocompatibility, low-cost and facile synthetic methods. Although many types of commercial chemicals have been used as precursors to produce C-dots, it is a challenge to obtain C-dots with good biocompatibility and brightness for textile applications. In this study, we produced colloidal C-dots by using Malabar spinach seeds juice (MSSJ) as a natural precursor, demonstrating the impact of the seeds' chemical composition on C-dots formation and performance. MSSJ contains carbohydrates, protein, glycerol, ethanol, chlorophyll as well as anthocyanin. Based on the variation in composition before and after C-dots synthesis, we determined that the structure of the C-dots is primarily derived from carbohydrates and protein. While chlorophyll remains stable during synthesis at 160 °C. The high nitrogen content from proteins enhances C-dots formation. The C-dots in this work have a high quantum yield of 7.8 %, and the emission peak range is 400-525 nm, which is significantly higher than C-dots derived from traditional biomass. Biological tests show no significant biotoxicity at any concentration, ensuring safe use in fluorescent textiles. This study underscores the importance of biomass-derived precursors in green C-dots synthesis, advancing the development of sustainable and biocompatible fluorescent fibers.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: C-dots are fluorescent nanomaterials, typically featuring sizes smaller than 10 nm (Bazazi et al., 2024). Compared to inorganic quantum dots, C-dots are composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), with metal-element-free structure. Due to their unique properties, such as composition-tunable optical properties, good biocompatibility, and easy synthesis using various types of approaches with earth-abundant precursors, to date, C-dots have been used as building blocks for various types of applications, including drug delivery (Singh et al., 2024; Wu et al., 2023), bio-imaging biosensor (Bosu, et al., 2023; Caires et al., 2020; Kwon & Rhee, 2012; Li et al., 2017; Li et al., 2021; Sarma & Mohanta, 2015), luminescent solar concentrators (Hosseinpanahi et al., 2024), light-emitting diodes (LEDs) (Chen, Wang, & Chen, 2012), photocatalysis (Jiliang et al., 2021; Prasannan & Imae, 2013; Singh et al., 2023), dyeing (Momina, Ahmad, & Khan, 2024), and fluorescent anti-counterfeiting (Ge et al., 2021; Li, Han, Wang, & Zhao, 2024; Li, Wang, et al., 2024).

Compared with commercial chemicals, C-dots can be easily synthesized using biomass materials, which are low-cost and renewable resources. For example, Liu et al. synthesized high-performance deep red emissive C-dots using taxus leaves (Junjun Liu et al., 2020). Chen et al. synthesized deep-red C-dots using clover as a precursor, with a narrow emission bandwidth of 21 nm (R. Chen et al., 2023). Owing to the straightforward synthesis of biomass-derived C-dots, the majority of researchers tend to focus on their direct preparation and the subsequent utilization of C-dots in potential applications, while there are relatively fewer endeavors aimed at elucidating the precise constituents within biomass and discover how the constituents contributed to the structure and properties of the C-dots. For instance, taxus leaves (J. Liu et al., 2020), blueberry (Aslandaş et al., 2015), sweet potatoes (Shen et al., 2017), and sugarcane juice (Mehta, Jha, & Kailasa, 2014) have been used for C-dots production, while it is lack of in-depth analysis of the key components and their impact on the structure and optical properties of C-dots. Moreover, the research on biocompatibility of the C-dots derived from the biomass was rarely conducted.

Malabar spinach is also known as Basella alba, which is a natural resource native to China with notable bioactive properties. This climbing plant, characterized by thick, spinach-like leaves, is widely used in Asian and African cuisines. Malabar spinach contains a significant amount of carbohydrates and high-quality protein, making it a good carbon source. However, Malabar spinach seeds have yet to be fully explored and utilized, despite their vigorous growth, low cost, and abundance.

In this study, using MSSJ as the raw material, we aim to systematically investigate the chemical composition in MSSJ and explored how to synthesize highly luminescent and biocompatible C-dots coupled with polysaccharides and protein. In details, we focus on the composition contribution of the MSSJ on the structure and optical properties of the C-dots. The structure of C-dots was analyzed in detail based on XPS and infrared spectroscopy. Finally, according to the optical performance, a biomass C-dots with high efficiency and easy production was selected, and the biocompatibility was analyzed. The composition changes of biomass before and after the synthesis of C-dots were analyzed by high performance liquid chromatography (HPLC), and C-dots were characterized and analyzed by XPS and FTIR. Finally, the synthesized point c is used for the dyeing of fluorescent fabrics.

Malabar spinach seeds were planted in Pingdu City, China. After 2-month growth, the dark seeds were collected for making the juice. Standard samples were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd., including the following reagents: Glucose (99.0 %), D-(+)-Xylose (98.0 %), D(-)-Arabinose, Ethanol (AR), Erythritol (99.0 %), Glycerol, Fructose (99.0 %), Maltose (95.0 %), Sulfuric Acid (98 %), crystalline bovine serum albumin (BSA), Coomassie Brilliant Blue G-250 staining solution, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS, pH 7.0). All reagents were used directly without further pretreatment in the current work.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of Malabar spinach seed juice
ce:section.ce:para: The collected dark seeds were repeatedly washed with distilled water. After washing, the seeds are ground to sludge state using a mortar. The sludge was then screened using a 400-mesh sieve to obtain the seed juice for subsequent experiments. The seed juice was then stored in the refrigerator for the next step.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of the Malabar spinach seed juice
ce:section.ce:para: The components of MSSJ were analyzed according to standard procedures (Table S2), using HPLC (Supporting Information). The content of disaccharides and monosaccharides can be directly tested by HPLC using MSSJ. However, for polysaccharides, an additional acid hydrolysis step is required (Supporting Information). The component concentrations in the sample were determined using the standard sample calibration curve (Fig. S8). Chlorophyll content was determined using a spectrophotometric method, calculated based on absorbance at 663 nm and 645 nm (Yu et al., 2024). Protein quantification was performed using the Bradford method (Karimi et al., 2022), which is based on the absorbance change resulting from the binding of Coomassie Brilliant Blue to the protein (Supporting Information).

Take 10 mL of MSSJ and place it in the hydrothermal reactor. Place hydrothermal reactors at 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C respectively. According to Fig. S11, the proportional amplification of the reaction volume does not change the optical properties of the C-dots. We compared four C-dots QYs and reaction phenomena. Next, we focused on studying the carbon points at 160 °C and 200 °C. The 160 °C treated samples (MSSJ 160 °C) were detected and two types of emission were found, which were from liquid C-dots dispersion and solid red fluorescent material. After 200 °C treatment (MSSJ 200 °C), only C-dots were obtained. After the reaction, the resulting C-dots solution was filtered using a filter and then transferred into a 500 Da dialysis bag for dialysis for 24 hours. Finally, the liquid retained inside the 500 Da dialysis bag was collected as the study sample and all characterized samples were purified solely through dialysis. with the samples labeled as C-dots (160 °C) and C-dots (200 °C) and stored at 4 °C for subsequent systematic characterization and analysis.
The scheme for analysis and preparation of the C-dots is shown in Fig. 1 .

For biocompatibility assessment, the RAW264.7 cells were seeded into 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 104 cells/well with the dulbecco's modified eagle medium (DMEM) containing 10 % fetal bovine serum (FBS). After culturing for 24 h in an incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO2), the normal medium was replaced with culture medium containing the C-dots filtered by 0.22 μm membrane with concentrations ranging from 0 to 500 μg/mL for 48 h incubation. The C-dots were before the test. The cell viability was assessed using the MTT assay, in which the absorbance readings were taken at 570 nm. For in vitro bioimaging, the cells were treated with the C-dots with 200 μg/mL in 35 mm glass bottom dishes. After 24 h incubation, the culture medium was removed, and the cells were rinsed with PBS gently. The cellular fluorescence images were acquired using a NIKON A1R HD25 confocal microscope with a 488 nm-wavelength laser.

The as-prepared C-dots were filtered twice using a 0.22 μm membrane filter, and the resulting transparent C-dots solution was utilized directly for dyeing process on knitted/weaved polyamide fabric. The fabric was cut to 10 × 10 cm size and washed before dyeing. The cleaned fabric was immersed in the C-dots solution for dyeing, with a controlled heating rate of 5 °C/min, and the dyeing was carried out at a constant temperature of 80 °C for 1 h. After dyeing, the fabric was removed, washed several times with running water, and then dried again. The resulting transparent mixture can also be used for pattern printing. We used a Canon TS3480 printer to apply the fluorescent ink onto non-fluorescent paper, and the results were satisfactory.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Component analysis of Malabar spinach seed juice
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of C-dots was carried out through hydrothermal treatment of MSSJ (as shown in Fig. 1). To investigate the influence of MSSJ chemical components on the production of C-dots, we analyzed the biomass before and after C-dots synthesis. The HPLC results displayed the variations of different components in various samples (Table 1 and Table S2). The MSSJ contained mostly water (96.2 %), and the other chemical components specifically include glucose, fructose, protein, glycerol, chlorophyll, anthocyanin, and a large number of polysaccharides such as glucan, xylan, and arabinan; the polysaccharide content (0.45 %) in MSSJ is slightly higher than monosaccharides (0.38 %). Unlike other similar research on biomass, the MSSJ contains a large number of protein, it is approximately 0.12 % protein detected in raw MSSJ, which is one fourth of polysaccharide content. During the preparation of C-dots, the carbohydrates and protein contents significantly decreased. At 160 °C, a small amount of carbohydrates remained, while another fluorescent material, chlorophyll, precipitated. At 200 °C, carbohydrates in the C-dots solution were entirely consumed, and subsequent analysis of the reaction precipitates confirmed the absence of residual carbohydrates or proteins, suggesting their participation in C-dots formation.

Chlorophyll was not detected in the MSSJ (160 °C) solution. Upon heating at 160 °C, fluorescence molecules underwent carbonization and polymerization to form C-dots while retaining their chromophore emission properties (Liang et al., 2023). The retained chlorophyll precipitated out due to its insolubility in water, making it undetectable. At 200 °C, increased carbonization led to the destruction of the chlorophyll molecular fluorophore. This further confirmed that the deep red emission at 160 °C originated from the fluorescent molecular center rather than carbon (R. Chen et al., et al., 2023).

Although MSSJ contains various components, it is primarily carbohydrates (polysaccharides and monosaccharides) that contribute to C-dots formation and determine C-dots structure and properties. Thus, we conducted a detailed investigation into the structure and performance of these C-dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Structure and optical properties of C-dots
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and size of the prepared C-dots were analyzed using transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. 2 a and 2d, the C-dots exhibit some aggregation and have a spherical morphology. The average particle size of the C-dots prepared at 160 °C is around 4.17 nm, with a measured d-spacing of approximately 0.31 nm (fig. S4a), whereas those synthesized at 200 °C were slightly larger, with an average size of 4.45 nm and a d-spacing of approximately 0.32 nm (Fig. S4b). TEM further confirmed that the C-dots exhibit well-defined lattice fringe with a spacing of 0.32 nm, corresponding to the (002) crystal plane of graphitic carbon (Duan et al., 2016; Ge et al., 2015; Tang et al., 2022). The presence of these clear lattice fringes suggests that the C-dots possess a crystalline structure, which likely results from enhanced carbonization at elevated synthetic temperatures.

A systematical investigation of the C-dots structure using XPS and FT-IR was conducted. XPS analysis (Fig. 2b) revealed that the hydrothermally synthesized C-dots (160 °C) are composed of C (65.1 %), N (11.83 %), and O (21.98 %). The peaks were identified as typical C1s (284.8 eV), N1s (399.3 eV), and O1s (532.8 eV). The C1s spectrum showed three distinct components (Fig. 2c), corresponding to CC/CC (284.8 eV), CO/CN (286.4 eV), and CO (288 eV) (Senol & Bozkurt, 2020). The high-resolution N1s band (Fig. S5b) can be decomposed as pyridinic N (399.7 eV) and graphitic N (401.9 eV) (Liu et al., 2024; Wang et al., 2020), and the high-resolution O1s band (Fig. S5a) could be deconvoluted into CO (531.2 eV) and C-O-C (532.5 eV) (Jin et al., 2024; Shi et al., 2021). By analyzing the XPS data, we did not find a significant variation for the N content in the final C-dots synthesized at different temperatures (Table S3). As shown in previous data, the MSSJ contains 0.12 % protein, which could be the primary source to provide the N in the C-dots. The C-dots prepared at 200 °C exhibited an increased degree of carbonization and tend a tendency to undergo slight aggregation, resulting in a slight increase in particle size (Fig. 2d). While the surface chemical groups did not undergo significant qualitative changes, only slight variations in their quantity compared to C-dots synthesized at 160 °C.

FT-IR analysis further supports the conclusions from XPS, revealing the surface functional groups of the C-dots, while HPLC indicates that these functional groups originate from carbohydrates and protein. It was confirmed that the C-dots are composed of C, N, and O, and it was also determined that their surface is functionalized by carboxyl (COOH), ether (CO), amino (NH2), hydroxyl (OH), and carbonyl (CO) groups (Zhao et al., 2022).

The as-produced C-dots have a typical absorption in the range of 200 to 500 nm, showing a pale-yellow color, and they appear transparent and clear under sunlight (Fig. 3 a). Both absorption curves of the C-dots exhibit strong absorption between 200 nm and 250 nm, which is likely related to the π-π* electronic transition of CC bonds (Edison et al., 2016; Zhang et al., 2016; Zhao et al., 2022). The absorption between 250 nm and 400 nm may be attributed to the n-π* transitions of NH and CO (Gao et al., 2018), respectively, especially associated with the oxidized functional groups on the C-dots. Compared to the 200 nm to 250 nm region, the absorption in the 250 nm to 400 nm range is relatively weaker. The fluorescence spectrum of the C-dots is shown in Fig. 3c and d. The prepared C-dots exhibit excitation-dependent fluorescence spectra, with the emission peak red-shifting as the excitation wavelength increases from 330 nm to 465 nm, concentrating in the visible region of the spectrum (from 400 nm to 525 nm). This phenomenon has been widely observed in C-dots derived from biomass because the low-carbonization of the core affects the energy state of the C-dots, and the fluorescence is mostly caused by surface groups, especially under different excitations. The existence of multiple emission centers caused by various surface defect states. FTIR and XPS analyses confirmed that using MSSJ biomass as the carbon source, a complex mixture of carbonaceous domains with different carbonization degrees and surface functional groups such as OH, COOH, and CO was formed. This leads to different energy levels and emission traps on the surface of C-dots. Therefore, electrons excited at different wavelengths relax through different radiation pathways, resulting in excitation-dependent fluorescence. This characteristic is consistent with the behavior of other bio-based C-dots reported previously (Chen et al., 2023; Liang et al., 2016; Ren et al., 2023). As shown in Fig. S10, C-dots synthesized via MSSJ from various regions exhibit no noticeable variations in their optical properties. The chlorophyll emission peak remained constant at 675 nm regardless of the excitation light (Fig. 3b), exhibiting bright red fluorescence (Fig. S9). The high temperature of 200 °C leads to an increased degree of carbonization, causing changes in the lattice structure and surface chemical properties of the C-dots. Over-carbonized C-dots may form more non-radiative recombination centers, which increase non-radiative energy dissipation, reduce photon emission, and ultimately result in a lower fluorescence quantum yield (QY). Infrared data also indicate that highly carbonized C-dots tend to contain fewer functional surface groups, such as hydroxyl and amine, which enhances the fluorescence performance of C-dots.

Furthermore, the QYs and lifetimes of the C-dots and chlorophyll were measured using an integrating sphere. We prepared a total of four groups of C-dots at 140 °C, 160 °C, 180 °C and 200 °C, and their quantum yields were 1.4 %, 7.8 %, 7.2 % and 1.7 % respectively (Fig. S12). The C-dots (160 °C) exhibited a lifetime of 1.30 ns (Fig. 4 a and b). The C-dots (200 °C) exhibited a QY of 1.7 % and a lifetime of 1.46 ns (Fig. S6a). The high QY of the C-dots produced at 160 °C could be due to the improved optimization between the core carbonization and the available surface functional groups, which contributed to the well-defined surface/core structure to largely avoid the surface defects induced PL quenching. Secondly, MSSJ contains proteins, and an appropriate nitrogen content provides new emission centers for C-dots, enhances electron transitions, and thus improves the QY. Chlorophyll obtained at 160 °C has a QY of 23.9 % and a lifetime of 6.54 ns, indicating rapid exciton recombination and minimal non-radiative recombination of the Chlorophyll compared to the C-dots (R. Chen, Wang, Pang, Teng, Li, Jiang, Zheng, Zhang, Zheng, Chen, et al., 2023).

Before investigating the fluorescent fibers fabrication and properties of C-dots, the assessment of toxicity and biocompatibility of C-dots was implemented. The cell viability of the C-dots with different concentrations (100–500 μg/mL) for 48 h incubation was observed through MTT assay (Fig. 5 a). The results show that as the concentration increases, cell viability remains relatively stable without significant fluctuations. Specifically, when the concentration of the C-dots is below 200 μg/mL, cell viability is over 95 %. Even at a concentration of 500 μg/mL, the cell viability is still 94 %. Additionally, to better observe the effect of C-dots on the cell morphologies, the confocal microscopy was used for cell imaging, in which the RAW 264.7 cells were cultured with the C-dots for 24 h. It was observed that the cells maintained their good morphology with intact cell membranes, and the C-dots could enter cells and were distributed in the cytoplasm. These results indicate that the as-prepared C-dots using MPSJ as a natural precursor could not affect the cell reproduction and exhibit good biocompatibility.

Zeta potential analysis showed that the C-dots had a negative surface charge and good stability (Fig. S3). Given the higher QY and broader emission band of C-dots (160 °C) compared to that of synthesized at 200 °C, we selected the 160 °C C-dots solution for preparing fluorescent patterns and fabric dyeing. As shown in Fig. 6 a, the dyed fabric appears colorless under ambient light. However, when illuminated at 365 nm and 395 nm, distinct fluorescence patterns emerged. Under indoor lighting, the fabric shows invisible fluorescence. Upon illumination at 365 nm, the fabric exhibits a strong, bright blue fluorescence, highlighting the C-dots' emission. At 395 nm, the fabric displays a bright green fluorescence. Once the excitation was turned off, the fluorescence patterns completely disappeared. As shown in Fig. 6b, we printed the designed pattern. It can be clearly seen that the unexcited paper does not show the pattern, and once it is excited, a clear fluorescent pattern can be seen. Under illumination at 365 nm, the clear bule pattern was found; while under 395 nm illumination, a green color was found, which is attributed to the excitation-dependent photoluminescence behavior of the C-dots.

This study demonstrates that C-dots synthesized from the natural precursor of MSSJ exhibit good fluorescent properties and commendable biocompatibility. The investigation into the influence of the chemical constituents of the seeds on both the formation and performance of C-dots revealed that carbohydrates predominantly, emitting vibrant green fluorescence, contribute to their structural integrity. The presence of protein content plays a decisive role in the improvement of QY. An appropriate nitrogen content provides new emission centers for carbon dots, enhances electron transitions, and thus improves the QY. This research addresses a significant knowledge gap concerning the utilization of natural precursors in C-dots synthesis, thereby enhancing the quality of biomass-derived C-dots. The C-dots produced at 160 °C achieve a high quantum yield of 7.8 % and display characteristic excitation-dependent emission spectra. Additionally, hydrothermal treatment at this temperature concurrently yields water-insoluble red fluorescent substances, confirmed to be chlorophyll. The chlorophyll extracted through hydrothermal methods exhibits an impressive quantum yield reaching up to 23.9 %, which emits vibrant red fluorescence. These two products are suitable for distinct applications; specifically, the synthesized C-dots can serve as fluorescent dyes for wearable textiles, indicating substantial market potential. However, this kind of exploration is only applicable to biomaterials with high sugar and protein content. Future research can be conducted by studying different types of seeds and extracting the effective components in their biomass, reducing the hindrance of seasonal changes to the progress of experiments.

Lihui Hou: Writing – original draft, Validation, Data curation. Shuping Ge: Visualization, Formal analysis. Weihua Li: Writing – review & editing, Methodology. Qiulian Liu: Conceptualization. Jin Liu: Conceptualization. Jingyi Li: Conceptualization. Changqing Qu: Funding acquisition. Yuanming Zhang: Funding acquisition. Haiguang Zhao: Writing – review & editing. Wei Jiang: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition. Yi Han: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: There are no conflicts to declare.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The Central Guidance on Local Science and Technology Development Fund of Shandong Province (YDZX2024023), Talent Introduction and Cultivation Plan for Key Industrial Parks in Xinjiang Autonomous Region, and Special Foundation of “Taishan Scholar” Construction Program (tsqn 202408165).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.carpta.2025.100888.
